title: Modern Paver Planter
summary: A cedar 2x2 frame that holds four concrete cap blocks as its walls — the wood is just the skeleton, the pavers are the planter.
description:
This one flips the usual material relationship: instead of wood walls with a stone accent, concrete paver caps ARE the walls, and the 2x2 cedar frame is just the structure that holds them upright and in place. It's a fast build and an unusual, genuinely modern look for very little lumber.

Measure your actual blocks before cutting the frame. Paver dimensions vary by manufacturer and even by batch, and the frame's slot width has to match your blocks with a small amount of clearance — about 1/8" to 1/4" — to slide them in without forcing them. Landscape adhesive between the wood and the blocks is what actually holds the assembly together long-term, and it needs a full cure (usually overnight) under light clamping pressure, like a ratchet strap, before the planter can be moved or loaded.
timeLabel: 3–4 hrs, plus overnight for the adhesive to cure

materials.name: 8x16x2 concrete cap blocks
materials.note: The planter's actual walls — measure yours before cutting the frame.

materials.name: Cedar, 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Pressure-treated works too if it's for exterior use.

materials.name: 2-1/2" exterior deck screws or pocket hole screws

cutList.note: Measure your blocks first and adjust — cut a little long to allow the blocks to slide in with about 1/8"–1/4" clearance.

steps.title: Build the two side frames
steps.body: Build two identical side frames from the legs and rails, either with predrilled 2-1/2" exterior deck screws and glue, or with pocket holes set for 1-1/2" stock and 2-1/2" exterior pocket hole screws. The top rail needs to sit down from the leg tops by a distance matching the width of your paver blocks — this is the slot they'll sit in.
materials: 2-1/2" exterior deck screws or pocket hole screws

steps.title: Tie the sides together
steps.body: Join the two side frames with more 2x2 rails using the same joinery method, forming the full box shape.

steps.title: Add internal support
steps.body: Repeat the process to add a few more 2x2 rails inside for extra rigidity, especially if your blocks are heavy or your frame spans a long side.

steps.title: Glue in the blocks and let it cure
steps.body: Apply landscape adhesive to the wood where each block will sit, then slide the blocks into place. Wrap a ratchet strap or rope around the whole assembly to hold everything tight while the adhesive cures — check the product's instructions, but expect to leave it overnight. Once cured, line the inside with landscape fabric, add soil, and plant.

images.alt: A modern planter box made from a cedar frame holding four concrete cap blocks as walls
